assessor_raw: The chest X-ray triage model has strong retrospective performance for targeted finding categories and the queueing simulation provides a mechanistically coherent argument for how worklist prioritization could reduce time-to-read for urgent studies. The model's underlying idea is sound: if urgent findings are moved earlier in the radiologist's queue, they get reported sooner. However, the memo's conclusion — that this constitutes a 'patient-outcome-improving intervention' — outpaces the evidence by at least one causal step. Patient outcomes depend not only on when the radiologist reads the study but on the entire downstream chain: clinician notification, bed and staffing availability, and treatment initiation. The simulation models none of these. Additionally, for the highest-acuity findings (large pneumothorax, misplaced lines), many hospitals have existing direct escalation protocols that bypass the standard worklist entirely — the model may be adding marginal value where the existing system already handles critical cases. The correct framing is: 'simulation supports a prospective deployment study to measure time-to-read and time-to-treatment impact.' The outcome-improving claim requires deployment evidence.

empirical_test.measure: prospective deployment: time-to-first-read; time-to-treatment; parallel escalation rate for flagged urgent cases
empirical_test.success_criterion: time-to-read and time-to-treatment both reduce; parallel escalation does not account for most gains
empirical_test.failure_criterion: time-to-read reduces but time-to-treatment unchanged; or parallel escalation already covers most high-urgency cases
